stem: A 9-month-old infant is brought to a regional emergency department by his parents with 3 days of coryza and cough, and 24 hours of increasing work of breathing. He has taken half his usual feeds today and had no apnoea. He was born at 39 weeks and has no comorbidities. The nearest paediatric retrieval team is 90 minutes away.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.6 degrees Celsius, HR 158, RR 62, SpO2 88% in room air, BP 92/58 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Moderate work of breathing with subcostal and intercostal recession, widespread fine crackles and expiratory wheeze, capillary refill 2 seconds, alert and consolable

lead_in: Which is the single best initial respiratory intervention?

options.text: Commence low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min titrated to maintain SpO2 at least 90% and admit under paediatrics for monitoring
options.rationale: The RCH Melbourne Bronchiolitis CPG specifies standard low-flow nasal prong oxygen as the first-line respiratory support, titrated to maintain SpO2 at least 90%, with HFNC reserved for low-flow failure (persistent SpO2 below 90% or no respiratory improvement after at least 2 hours). (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 to target SpO2 92 to 96% as first-line respiratory support
options.rationale: Would be correct only after a documented failure of low-flow oxygen (persistent SpO2 below 90% or no improvement after at least 2 hours); the 92 to 96% target is also a NICE figure rather than the RCH 90% target. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg with oxygen and reassess in 20 minutes for any change in work of breathing
options.rationale: The RCH CPG advises against routine salbutamol in bronchiolitis as it does not alter disease course. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 with continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring in the resuscitation cubicle
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is not part of routine bronchiolitis care per the RCH CPG; it is used for severe croup, not bronchiolitis. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Intubate, ventilate, and arrange urgent retrieval to a paediatric intensive care unit for further respiratory support
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for failure of escalating non-invasive support; the RCH ladder is low-flow first, then HFNC if low-flow fails, with PICU input before intubation. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: The RCH Melbourne Bronchiolitis CPG uses standard low-flow nasal prong oxygen as the first-line respiratory support, titrated to maintain SpO2 at least 90%. HFNC is reserved for low-flow failure (persistent SpO2 below 90% or no improvement after at least 2 hours). Salbutamol, adrenaline, and corticosteroids are not routinely used.
key_points: RCH first-line respiratory support: low-flow nasal prong oxygen
Target SpO2 at least 90% (not the NICE 92%)
HFNC reserved for documented low-flow failure
No routine salbutamol, adrenaline or corticosteroids in bronchiolitis
explanation.pearls: Brief desaturations below 90% alone are not an indication to start supplemental oxygen per the RCH CPG.

stem: A 4-year-old girl presents to her general practitioner with 4 months of infrequent, hard, painful bowel motions every 4 to 5 days. She withholds on the toilet and has occasional faecal soiling. There is no blood, no vomiting and her growth is on the 50th centile. Toilet training is otherwise complete. Coeliac serology and thyroid function are normal.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen with a palpable suprapubic faecal mass, normal anal position and tone, no anal fissure, normal lumbosacral spine
data_table.Investigations: Coeliac IgA tTG negative, TSH 2.4 mU/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Sodium phosphate enema with manual disimpaction under conscious sedation
options.rationale: Enemas and rectal interventions are distressing in preschoolers and are second-line after oral macrogol failure; they can also cause hyperphosphataemia in small children. (Source: NICE NG99)

options.text: Lactulose 5 mL orally twice daily as the sole maintenance therapy now
options.rationale: Lactulose is an acceptable maintenance osmotic agent if macrogol is not tolerated, but is less effective than macrogol for disimpaction and would not clear a palpable faecal mass. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Oral macrogol 3350 with electrolytes daily in escalating disimpaction dose
options.rationale: Oral macrogol 3350 with electrolytes is first-line disimpaction in Australian and NICE paediatric constipation pathways; doses are escalated daily until a clear watery stool is produced. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Increased dietary fibre and fluid alongside daily toileting routine review
options.rationale: Lifestyle measures are essential adjuncts and form the maintenance backbone, but cannot disimpact a palpable retained faecal mass and will fail without pharmacological clearance first. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Anorectal manometry and paediatric surgery referral for suction biopsy
options.rationale: Suction rectal biopsy for Hirschsprung disease is indicated when red flags are present such as delayed meconium, failure to thrive or abnormal anal tone, none of which apply here. (Source: NICE NG99)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation in preschoolers with a palpable faecal mass requires disimpaction before maintenance therapy will work. Oral macrogol 3350 with electrolytes is first-line in Australian pathways. Maintenance follows with a lower macrogol dose plus behavioural toileting and fibre. Red flags for organic disease include delayed meconium beyond 48 hours, ribbon stools, failure to thrive, abnormal lumbosacral examination and abnormal anal tone.
key_points: Disimpaction precedes maintenance in retained faecal mass
Macrogol 3350 with electrolytes oral disimpaction is first-line
Behavioural toileting and fibre are essential maintenance adjuncts, not stand-alone disimpaction
explanation.pearls: Macrogol disimpaction in a 4-year-old typically starts at 4 sachets daily and escalates by 2 sachets per day to a maximum around 8 sachets, continued until a clear watery stool.

stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department at 0200 with a 1-day history of coryza followed by 4 hours of a barking cough and noisy breathing. He is alert, drinking small sips, and pinker after being upset on triage. He has had no foreign body exposure and his immunisations are up to date.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.2 degrees Celsius, HR 140, RR 36, SpO2 96% in room air
data_table.Examination: Inspiratory stridor at rest, moderate suprasternal and intercostal recession, barking cough, no drooling, chest clear, alert

lead_in: What is the single best initial pharmacological management?

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose; observe until stridor at rest settles and oral intake is adequate (typically 1-2 hours)
options.rationale: Moderate croup (stridor at rest with moderate recession, alert, drinking, pink) is treated per RCH with oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg; the 0.6 mg/kg dose and nebulised adrenaline are reserved for severe or life-threatening disease. Observation is tied to clinical resolution rather than a fixed clock; peak dexamethasone effect is 1-2 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Intramuscular dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg plus paracetamol 15 mg/kg orally with at least 3 hours of in-department observation
options.rationale: Intramuscular dexamethasone is reserved for vomiting or oral intolerance; the 0.6 mg/kg dose is reserved for severe or life-threatening croup, not moderate disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised budesonide 2 mg as a single dose and arrange discharge home after consultant review and parent education
options.rationale: Nebulised budesonide is reserved for children who cannot tolerate oral dexamethasone; oral dexamethasone is preferred for cost, efficacy, and ease of dosing. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg plus nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 flat dose with at least 3 hours of post-adrenaline observation
options.rationale: The 0.6 mg/kg dexamethasone dose plus nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe or life-threatening croup (marked recession, agitation, exhaustion, or hypoxia); this child is alert, drinking, and pink after being upset, which is moderate disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Intubation in theatre with paediatric intensive care unit admission for ongoing airway and respiratory support after retrieval
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for impending respiratory failure with exhaustion, cyanosis, or altered consciousness; moderate croup almost always responds to oral dexamethasone alone. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Per the RCH croup guideline, dexamethasone dosing is graded to severity: 0.15 mg/kg orally for mild-to-moderate disease (stridor at rest with mild-to-moderate recession, alert and well perfused), and 0.6 mg/kg (maximum 12 mg) for severe or life-threatening disease (marked recession, agitation, exhaustion, hypoxia, or altered consciousness). Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe or life-threatening croup at a flat dose of 5 mL of 1:1000, with a minimum 3-hour observation afterwards because effects last 30 to 60 minutes. After dexamethasone alone, observation is tied to clinical resolution of stridor at rest rather than a fixed 4-hour clock.
key_points: Mild-to-moderate croup: dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg orally per RCH
Severe or life-threatening croup: dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg plus nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000
Nebulised adrenaline observation is 3 hours post-dose per RCH
After dexamethasone alone, observe to clinical resolution (peak effect 1-2 hours)
explanation.pearls: Per-kg adrenaline dosing (0.5 mL/kg of 1:1000) is the older AAP formulation; RCH uses a flat 5 mL of 1:1000 dose regardless of weight for severe or life-threatening croup.

stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with 3 days of palpable purpura over his buttocks and lower legs, bilateral ankle swelling, and crampy peri-umbilical abdominal pain that has worsened in the last 12 hours. He has vomited twice this morning. His mother reports no haematemesis or melaena. He had a sore throat 2 weeks ago.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.4 degrees Celsius, HR 110, RR 22, BP 102/64 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Palpable non-blanching purpura on extensor surfaces and buttocks, soft abdomen with peri-umbilical tenderness, no peritonism, no scrotal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick blood 1+ protein negative, FBC platelets 320, creatinine 38 micromol/L, faecal occult blood positive

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with paracetamol and general practitioner review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Crampy abdominal pain with vomiting and positive faecal occult blood in HSP raises concern for intussusception or ischaemic bowel and warrants inpatient observation rather than community follow-up. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

options.text: Arrange urgent contrast computed tomography of abdomen to exclude appendicitis
options.rationale: CT exposes a child to substantial ionising radiation and is not first-line for suspected HSP-associated intussusception; ultrasound is the preferred initial imaging modality in paediatric abdominal pain. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg and arrange general practitioner follow-up
options.rationale: Steroids can reduce abdominal pain duration but should not be initiated in the community in a child with vomiting and occult blood until intussusception has been excluded. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

options.text: Refer urgently to nephrology for renal biopsy and immunosuppression today
options.rationale: Urine dipstick blood of 1+ without proteinuria, hypertension or renal impairment does not meet criteria for urgent nephrology referral; renal involvement is monitored with weekly urinalysis and blood pressure for 6 months. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

options.text: Admit for analgesia, oral hydration, surgical review and abdominal ultrasound
options.rationale: Significant abdominal pain with vomiting and occult blood is an indication for admission in HSP; ultrasound is the imaging of choice to assess for ileo-ileal intussusception, which is the typical HSP-associated pattern. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

explanation.summary: HSP is the most common childhood vasculitis with classic palpable purpura, arthralgia, abdominal pain and renal involvement. Admission criteria include severe abdominal pain, vomiting, GI bleeding, scrotal involvement, marked joint pain limiting mobility, or renal complications (hypertension, significant proteinuria, AKI). Ileo-ileal intussusception is the feared GI complication, evaluated with ultrasound. Most renal disease is mild and managed with 6 months of urinalysis and BP surveillance.
key_points: Admission triggers in HSP: severe abdominal pain, vomiting, GI bleed, scrotal involvement, renal flags
Ileo-ileal intussusception is the HSP-specific GI complication, evaluated with ultrasound
Renal monitoring: weekly urinalysis and BP for 6 months from onset
explanation.pearls: HSP intussusception is typically ileo-ileal rather than ileo-colic, which is why air enema reduction is less successful and ultrasound is the diagnostic modality of choice.

stem: A 3-year-old girl was admitted 36 hours ago with day-6 fever, polymorphous rash, bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue and unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. She received IVIG 2 g/kg and high-dose aspirin. She remains febrile to 39.1 degrees Celsius 36 hours after the IVIG infusion finished. Her echocardiogram performed today is normal. She has no allergies and the cardiology registrar has been notified.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.1 degrees Celsius, HR 150, RR 28, BP 90/52 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Persistent conjunctivitis and rash, irritable but consolable, no respiratory distress, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: CRP 95 mg/L (was 130 on admission), platelets 480, normal LFTs, blood culture pending and no growth at 24 hours

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer a second dose of IVIG 2 g/kg over 10 to 12 hours
options.rationale: Refractory Kawasaki disease is defined as persistent or recrudescent fever 36 hours after the first IVIG; current Australian and AHA practice is a second IVIG 2 g/kg as the first re-treatment step, with cardiology supervision. (Source: AHA Scientific Statement: Kawasaki 2017)

options.text: Commence intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg daily for 3 days
options.rationale: IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy is reserved for IVIG-resistant disease that has already failed a second IVIG, or for very high-risk patients at initial diagnosis under specialist guidance. (Source: AHA Scientific Statement: Kawasaki 2017)

options.text: Start intravenous infliximab 5 mg/kg as a single infusion
options.rationale: Infliximab is a third-line option for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki after a second IVIG has failed; using it now would skip the recommended re-treatment sequence. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki)

options.text: Escalate aspirin from anti-inflammatory to antiplatelet dose
options.rationale: Aspirin dose is reduced to antiplatelet dosing (3 to 5 mg/kg) after 48 to 72 hours afebrile, not while the child is still febrile and inflamed. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki)

options.text: Stop aspirin and start intravenous flucloxacillin and ceftriaxone
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics for cervical lymphadenopathy are inappropriate when blood culture is negative, the clinical picture meets Kawasaki criteria and IVIG has already produced a partial CRP fall. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki)

explanation.summary: Around 10 to 20% of Kawasaki disease is IVIG-resistant, defined as persistent or recrudescent fever at least 36 hours after the IVIG infusion ends. First re-treatment is a second IVIG 2 g/kg in Australian protocols. If still resistant, options are IV methylprednisolone pulse or infliximab, chosen with cardiology guidance and based on coronary risk. Aspirin remains high-dose anti-inflammatory until 48 to 72 hours afebrile, then reduced to antiplatelet dosing. Repeat echocardiogram at 2 weeks and 6 to 8 weeks regardless.
key_points: IVIG resistance: persistent or recrudescent fever 36 hours after first IVIG
Second IVIG 2 g/kg is standard first re-treatment in Australian practice
Pulse methylprednisolone or infliximab follows if second IVIG fails
explanation.pearls: Kobayashi score predicts IVIG resistance in Japanese cohorts; western validation is weaker, so the AHA pathway still uses clinical fever response 36 hours post-IVIG as the primary trigger for re-treatment.
